summary: Ronacher explains why MiniJinja’s dynamic runtime values need reference counting and dynamic dispatch that Rust’s object safety rules block. He shows that an `Object` trait with `&Arc<Self>` receivers enables refcount-aware behavior but prevents building a standard trait object. To solve this, he constructs a custom fat pointer and vtable via a `type_erase!` macro that generates trampolines, refcount hooks, and downcasting support. The post walks through the unsafe machinery and the macro that produces it, then integrates the result into MiniJinja’s `Value` representation. He concludes that Rust’s restrictions can be worked around with creativity and unsafe code, and argues that better compile-time introspection would make such patterns far less contorted.
key_points: MiniJinja needs dynamic, GC-like value behavior in Rust, best approximated with reference counting and dynamic dispatch.
Using `&Arc<Self>` in the trait API enables self-cloning and iterator lifetimes but breaks object safety for `Arc<dyn Object>`.
A custom vtable and erased pointer (`DynObject`) restore dynamic dispatch, including downcasting and refcount management.
A macro generates the unsafe glue code and trampolines to make the pattern reusable.
The exercise highlights Rust’s macro limitations and motivates compile-time introspection features.
money_quotes: Given that building programming languages and interpreters is the developer's most favorite hobby, I will never stop writing templating engines.
The reason it cannot be made into an object is because we declare the receiver as `&Arc<Self>` instead of `&Self`.
A vtable is nothing more than a struct that holds a field with a function pointer for each method on the trait.
Since this requires a lot of unsafe code, and we want to generate all the necessary trampolines to put into the vtable automatically, we will use a macro.
The macro is surprisingly only a bit awful.
Just because Rust tells you that you cannot make something into an object does not mean that you actually can't.
It just requires some creativity and the willingness to actually use unsafe code.
Zig programmers will laugh / cry about this since comptime is a much more powerful system to make something like this work compared to the ridiculous macro abuse necessary in Rust.
key_insight: When Rust’s object safety rules block dynamic patterns, you can recover the needed behavior with a custom vtable and macro-generated unsafe glue—though it exposes how much the language lacks ergonomic compile-time introspection.
